analogy: Think of "Physical and Chemical Changes" through the chapter's own opening images: Respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year. He was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop. He ran fast and reached the... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Physical and Chemical Changes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year.

The text explains that he was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop.

The text explains that he ran fast and reached the bus-stop in a few minutes.

The text explains that his grandmother asked him why he was breathing so fast.

The text explains that boojho told her that he came running all the way.

The text explains that he wondered why running makes a person breathe faster.

The text explains that the answer to Boojho’s question lies in understanding why we breathe.

The text explains that in Chapter 2 you learnt that all organisms are made of small microscopic units called cells.

The text explains that a cell is the smallest structural and functional unit of an organism.

The text explains that each cell of an organism performs certain functions such as nutrition, transport, excretion and reproduction.

The text explains that to perform these functions, the cell needs energy.

The text explains that even when we are eating, sleeping or reading we require energy.

The text explains that can you say why your parents insist that you should eat regularly?

The text explains that the food has stored energy, which is released during respiration.

The text explains that therefore, all living organisms respire to get energy from food.

The text explains that we breathe out air which is rich in carbon dioxide.

The text explains that these words may be present in any direction — upwards, downwards, or along the diagonals.

The text explains that (c) The temperature of air is higher than that on the ground.

The text explains that (d) The pressure of air is higher than that on the ground.

The text explains that you can also collect material on this topic from other sources.

The text explains that you can seek help of your teacher or parents.

The text explains that find out the percentage of people of your area who smoke.

The text explains that if you have a smoker in your family, confront him with the material that you have collected.

The text explains that ask the doctor: (a) When does a person need artificial respiration?

The text explains that (b) Does the person need to be kept on artificial respiration temporarily or permanently?

The text explains that (c) From where can the person get supply of oxygen for artificial respiration?

The text explains that measure the breathing rate of the members of your family and some of your friends.

The text explains that investigate: (c) If the breathing rate of children is different from that of adults.

The text explains that (d) If the breathing rate of males is different from that of females.

The text explains that if there is a difference in any of these cases, try to find the reason.

Another source point is that respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year.

Another source point is that he was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop.

Another source point is that he ran fast and reached the bus-stop in a few minutes.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 6: Physical and Chemical Changes

prelims_traps: Do not treat 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE? and SCIENCE 58 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Physical and Chemical Changes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Physical and Chemical Changes through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE? and SCIENCE 58 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Physical and Chemical Changes, which statement is supported by the source text about 6.1 WHY DO WE RESPIRE??
options: Respiration in Organisms 6 O ne day Boojho was eagerly waiting to meet his grandparents who were coming to the town after a year.
He was in a real hurry as he wanted to receive them at the bus-stop.
His grandmother asked him why he was breathing so fast.
The answer to Boojho’s question lies in understanding why we breathe.
mcqs.trap_explanation: The distractors are also chapter-linked statements, but they do not answer the specific concept asked in the stem.
mcqs.approach_technique: Identify the concept in the stem, then match it to the statement with the same source context.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Physical and Chemical Changes, which statement is supported by the source text about CAUTION?
options: In the cells, oxygen in the air helps in the breakdown of food.
The process of breakdown of food in the cell with the release of energy is called cellular respiration.
Cellular respiration takes place in the cells of all organisms.
In the cell, the food (glucose) is broken down into carbon dioxide and water using oxygen.

options: When breakdown of glucose occurs with the use of oxygen it is called aerobic respiration.
